answers.value:
I attended my first A.A. meeting on June 17, 2007, and I have maintained continuous sobriety since June 18, 2007. My home group is the "West Side Serenity" group in Manhattan, which meets on Tuesday and Thursday evenings at a community center on the Upper West Side. I have been an active member of this home group for over 10 years and currently serve as the group’s General Service Representative (GSR).

Current A.A. Experience:
- Home Group Member (West Side Serenity Group, New York, NY): I attend this group regularly (3–4 times per week) and participate in group conscience meetings, business meetings, and service activities. I sponsor several men, most of whom are in early recovery, and I stay actively involved in step work, traditions meetings, and service workshops organized by our district and area.
- General Service Representative (GSR), District 10, Area 49 (SENY): Elected in 2022, I represent my home group at district and area meetings. I regularly report back to my group on area and conference-related matters, including literature changes, service opportunities, and agenda items for the General Service Conference. I also help coordinate group participation in area assemblies and encourage members to get involved in service beyond the group level.
- District Corrections Committee Member: I currently serve on our district’s corrections committee, helping coordinate A.A. meetings at a local detention center in Manhattan. I participate in a rotation of volunteers who bring A.A. meetings into the facility twice per month. My responsibilities include coordinating literature, maintaining contact with facility staff, and ensuring adherence to both A.A. Traditions and institutional guidelines.

Past A.A. Service:
- Group Secretary (2010–2012): At my original home group in Brooklyn ("Sunrise Beginners Group"), I served as group secretary for two years. I was responsible for opening and closing the meeting space, maintaining the meeting format, organizing chairpersons, and ensuring that 7th Tradition funds were properly counted and distributed according to group conscience.
- Group Treasurer (2012–2014): After moving to Manhattan and joining my current home group, I served as treasurer for a two-year rotation. I set up a transparent, simple accounting system, provided monthly financial reports to the group, and oversaw regular contributions to the district, area, and G.S.O., following the group’s conscience and A.A. guidelines.
- Intergroup Representative (2014–2016): I represented my group at the local intergroup association. I participated in monthly meetings, shared group announcements, coordinated volunteers for the local hotline, and helped organize a city-wide A.A. anniversary event. I also supported outreach efforts to hospitals and treatment centers by helping maintain the meeting list and volunteering as a contact for newcomers.
- District Committee Member (DCM), District 10, Area 49 (2016–2018): I was elected DCM and served a full two-year term. In this role, I helped coordinate several district workshops on topics such as the Twelve Concepts, safety in A.A., and singleness of purpose. I worked closely with GSRs from multiple groups, encouraging participation in area assemblies and fostering a spirit of unity and inclusivity across the district.
- Area 49 Committee Service: I served on the Area 49 Literature Committee (2018–2020), reviewing conference literature proposals, gathering group feedback, and assisting with literature displays at area events. I also participated in a temporary ad hoc committee focused on improving hybrid meeting access across the area during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Conference and Regional Participation:
- Delegate’s Sharing Sessions: While I have not served as a delegate, I have actively participated in pre-conference and post-conference assemblies, providing informed group conscience on agenda items related to literature, accessibility, and technology. I have attended several Northeast Regional Forums and have engaged in sharing sessions with trustees, staff members, and other trusted servants.

Sponsorship and Service to the Fellowship:
- Sponsorship: I have been sponsoring other alcoholics since around my third year of sobriety. I typically sponsor 3–5 men at a time, meeting weekly or bi-weekly, working through the Twelve Steps, and encouraging participation in service. Many of the men I sponsor have become active in general service and corrections work. I emphasize the importance of the Traditions and Concepts, particularly in how they relate to service beyond the group level.
- Workshop and Panel Participation: I have chaired and participated in numerous panels and workshops at the district and area level on topics such as the Traditions, Concepts, service sponsorship, and the role of G.S.R.s. I have also shared my experience on panels addressing anonymity in the digital age, A.A. and technology, and carrying the message into correctional and treatment facilities.

Throughout my A.A. journey, I have tried to approach service with humility, a willingness to listen, and a commitment to group conscience. My experience at the group, district, and area levels has given me a practical understanding of the A.A. Service Manual, the Twelve Traditions, and the Twelve Concepts for World Service, and has reinforced my belief that our primary purpose—to carry the message to the alcoholic who still suffers—must remain at the center of all our actions.

answers.value:
I have over 25 years of professional experience, primarily in nonprofit management, program development, and project leadership, with a significant focus on behavioral health, community services, and organizational effectiveness.

Current Position:
- Director of Program Operations, New Horizons Community Recovery Center, New York, NY (2016–present)
  In my current role, I oversee day-to-day operations for a medium-sized nonprofit organization that provides outpatient recovery support, peer-led groups, and community-based services for individuals with substance use disorders and co-occurring mental health conditions. I manage a team of approximately 35 staff members, including program managers, clinicians, peer specialists, and administrative personnel.

  Key responsibilities:
  - Strategic Planning and Implementation: Collaborate with the Executive Director and Board to develop and implement multi-year strategic plans, annual program goals, and key performance indicators.
  - Budget and Financial Oversight: Help develop and manage an annual operating budget of approximately $4.5 million, ensuring programs remain financially sustainable and aligned with funder requirements. Work closely with the finance team to monitor expenditures and adjust programs as needed.
  - Program Development and Evaluation: Lead the design, implementation, and evaluation of new and existing programs, including recovery coaching, family support groups, and reentry services for individuals leaving incarceration. Use data collection tools and outcome measures to assess effectiveness and report to funders.
  - Staff Supervision and Development: Directly supervise five program managers and indirectly oversee all program staff. Conduct performance reviews, provide coaching and mentoring, and organize ongoing training on topics such as trauma-informed care, ethics, and cultural humility.
  - Stakeholder and Community Relations: Maintain relationships with partner agencies, government funders, hospitals, and community organizations. Represent the organization at city and state-level behavioral health coalitions and task forces.

Previous Positions:
- Program Manager, Urban Pathways Behavioral Health Services, Brooklyn, NY (2010–2016)
  Managed a portfolio of outpatient and community-based programs, including a drop-in center, intensive outpatient groups, and vocational support services for individuals in recovery.
  - Coordinated multidisciplinary teams (social workers, counselors, case managers) to deliver integrated services.
  - Developed and implemented program policies, procedures, and documentation standards.
  - Led quality improvement initiatives that reduced client no-show rates and improved retention in services.
  - Worked closely with clinical directors to ensure compliance with state regulations and accreditation standards.

- Senior Case Manager, Hope House Transitional Living, New York, NY (2005–2010)
  Provided case management services in a transitional housing program for individuals exiting residential treatment and correctional facilities.
  - Assisted clients with housing, employment, education, and linkage to outpatient treatment and mutual-help groups.
  - Facilitated life-skills workshops, including budgeting, relapse prevention, and communication skills.
  - Developed a resource guide for clients that included information on local A.A. and other mutual-help meetings.

- Earlier Career (Pre-Sobriety and Early Sobriety):
  Before entering the behavioral health and nonprofit sectors, I worked in a variety of administrative and customer service roles, including administrative assistant positions at a small law firm and a property management company. These jobs gave me experience with basic office operations, client communication, and time management. My drinking during that period limited my effectiveness, and I ultimately left those positions before getting sober in 2007.

Relevant Skills and Professional Strengths:
- Organizational Leadership: Extensive experience managing multi-program operations, supporting staff development, and implementing strategic initiatives in alignment with organizational missions and values.
- Project Management: Certified Project Management Professional (PMP) with a track record of launching new programs, implementing new data systems, and coordinating multi-stakeholder projects on time and within budget.
- Financial and Contract Management: Familiarity with grant management, government contracts, and nonprofit budgeting, including preparing reports for city and state agencies and private foundations.
- Policy and Governance: Regularly work with nonprofit boards and advisory committees, helping them understand operational realities, interpret data, and make informed decisions. Comfortable reading and applying bylaws, policies, and governance documents.
- Communication and Facilitation: Experienced in facilitating meetings, leading cross-functional teams, and presenting to boards, funders, and community groups. Skilled at translating complex information into accessible language for diverse audiences.

My occupational background has given me practical experience with board relations, governance structures, and the unique challenges faced by organizations that must balance mission, financial sustainability, and accountability to stakeholders. This experience is directly relevant to service on the A.A. World Services Board, where thoughtful oversight, collaboration, and adherence to spiritual principles are essential.

answers.field_label: Additional Abilities Skills Background And Life Experiences That May Benefit The Aa World Services Board
answers.value:
Skills and Abilities:
- Nonprofit Governance and Board Relations: Over the last decade, I have regularly attended board meetings in my professional role, presented program reports, and worked closely with board committees (e.g., Program, Finance, and Governance). I understand the distinction between governance and management, and the importance of maintaining appropriate boundaries while still providing boards with the information they need to make informed decisions.

- Strategic Planning and Organizational Change: I have led or co-led several strategic planning processes involving staff, clients, and board members. This work has taught me how to facilitate group decision-making, balance multiple perspectives, and keep the mission at the center. I have experience guiding organizations through change, including implementing new technologies, restructuring programs, and adapting to funding shifts.

- Data-Informed Decision-Making: I am comfortable using data to inform decisions without losing sight of the human and spiritual aspects of our work. I have experience creating dashboards, tracking key performance indicators, and presenting data in a way that helps stakeholders understand trends and implications. I believe this skill can help the A.A. World Services Board evaluate projects and initiatives while remaining faithful to our primary purpose and spiritual principles.

- Communication, Writing, and Editing: I regularly draft grant proposals, program reports, policies, and training materials. I have a strong command of written English and an eye for clarity and consistency. I believe these skills could be useful in reviewing service materials, participating in committee work, and contributing to clear communication between the board, the Fellowship, and the General Service Office.

- Facilitation and Conflict Resolution: In both my professional and A.A. service roles, I have frequently facilitated meetings where strong emotions, differing opinions, and limited time were factors. I have experience using structured agendas, ground rules, and spiritual principles to help groups move toward an informed group conscience. I am comfortable listening to minority viewpoints and encouraging constructive discussion.

- Technology and Remote Collaboration: I have been involved in implementing hybrid and virtual meeting formats in both my workplace and my A.A. district. I am familiar with common videoconferencing platforms, collaborative tools, and basic cybersecurity considerations. I understand both the opportunities and challenges that technology presents for A.A., particularly regarding accessibility, anonymity, and inclusivity.

Personal and Life Experiences:
- Long-Term Sobriety with Ongoing Involvement: Having been sober since 2007, I have experienced A.A. through different phases of my life—early recovery, rebuilding my career, forming healthy relationships, and navigating loss and change without alcohol. I have remained actively involved in A.A. throughout, including during challenging periods such as the COVID-19 pandemic, when meetings moved online and many members struggled with isolation.

- Family Background and Diversity: I was raised in a working-class Puerto Rican family in the Bronx. Spanish was spoken in my grandparents’ home, and I grew up navigating multiple cultural contexts. This background has given me an appreciation for the importance of making A.A. accessible and welcoming to people of diverse backgrounds, including those who may feel marginalized or unsure if they belong.

- Lived Experience with the Criminal Justice and Treatment Systems: Prior to getting sober, I had several encounters with the legal system related to alcohol, including a DWI and a short period of probation. I also attended mandated outpatient treatment, where I was first introduced to A.A. Although I did not get sober immediately, these experiences gave me insight into the challenges faced by people who come into A.A. through courts, treatment, or other institutions. My later work in corrections-related A.A. service and reentry programs builds on this lived experience.

- Bilingual and Cross-Cultural Sensitivity: While English is my primary language, I speak conversational Spanish and have participated in some bilingual A.A. meetings. I am sensitive to the needs of members who may feel more comfortable sharing in their first language and to the importance of making A.A. literature and services accessible across language and cultural barriers.

- Experience with Illness, Loss, and Caregiving in Sobriety: During my sobriety, I have cared for an ailing parent and experienced the loss of close family members. I have walked through these experiences with the support of A.A., my sponsor, and my home group, learning firsthand how our program and fellowship can sustain us through grief and stress. This has deepened my appreciation for the spiritual foundations of our Traditions and Concepts.

Approach and Personal Qualities:
- Humility and Willingness to Learn: I do not view myself as an expert on A.A., but rather as a member who has been given opportunities to serve. I am aware that service at the world level requires a willingness to listen carefully, to seek guidance from group conscience, and to place principles before personalities.

- Respect for the Traditions and Concepts: My service experience has convinced me that A.A.’s Traditions and Concepts are essential to our unity and survival. I take them seriously and strive to apply them in practical ways, especially in matters of money, property, and authority.

- Ability to Work Collaboratively: I have experience working on multidisciplinary teams and in environments where collaboration is essential. I am comfortable working with individuals who have different viewpoints, communication styles, and backgrounds, and I strive to treat others with respect and patience.

I believe that this combination of professional skills, life experiences, and A.A. service can be of value to the A.A. World Services Board, particularly in areas related to governance, strategic planning, communications, and ensuring that our services remain accessible and responsive to the needs of the Fellowship worldwide.

If selected to serve, I am fully willing to disclose any relevant past interactions in more detail and to adhere to any conflict-of-interest policies that may apply.

persona.background_context:
This is Michael, a 51‑year‑old New Yorker who has been sober since June 18, 2007 and has built a life that weaves together recovery, family, and demanding nonprofit work. He lives on the Upper West Side of Manhattan in a rent-stabilized one‑bedroom that he shares with his partner, Rachel, and her nine‑year‑old son, Mikey, who calls him “Mike” or sometimes “Mikey Two” to distinguish him from himself. The apartment is a typical prewar walk‑up—scuffed hardwood floors, overstuffed bookshelves with recovery literature alongside sociology and project management texts, and a small desk in the corner that doubles as his home office. Most mornings start early: he’s up around 5:45 a.m., makes coffee, reads a daily meditation and a page from the Big Book or Twelve and Twelve, and checks in with a sponsee by text before waking Mikey for school.

Professionally, Michael has been Director of Program Operations at New Horizons Community Recovery Center in Manhattan since 2016, overseeing about 35 staff and an annual budget of roughly $4.5 million. His weekdays are a mix of strategy meetings with the Executive Director, supervision sessions with program managers, and troubleshooting everything from a peer specialist calling out sick to an issue with the new electronic records system. Twice a week he works late to attend community coalition meetings or city task forces on behavioral health and reentry; on those nights, Rachel handles dinner and homework. He’s proud that a reentry program he helped design in 2021 for individuals leaving Rikers has started to show strong outcomes, but he worries about looming state funding cuts and spends a lot of time in early 2026 refining dashboards and outcome reports to make the case to funders. He still carries some quiet anxiety about burnout—after a tough stretch during the height of COVID when the center went hybrid, he’s more intentional about boundaries, trying not to answer emails after 9 p.m. and scheduling one Friday afternoon a month as a “quiet desk day” for planning rather than back‑to‑back meetings.

Recovery and family life shape the rest of his week. His home group, West Side Serenity, meets nearby on Tuesday and Thursday evenings, and he’s there most weeks either for the regular meeting or a group conscience or district service commitment. As GSR, he walks down to district meetings in Midtown once a month, often grabbing a quick slice of pizza with another GSR on the way. He sponsors three men at the moment—two he meets with weekly at a coffee shop near Penn Station, and one he visits monthly at a Manhattan detention center as part of the district corrections committee rotation. Some Saturdays he heads to the Bronx to take his mother, Carmen, grocery shopping and then over to visit his 93‑year‑old grandmother at an assisted living facility; on those drives he’s reminded of his own pre‑sobriety DWI and the probation that first pushed him toward A.A. In the evenings, he and Rachel decompress over simple dinners—rice and beans, baked chicken, or takeout—while helping Mikey with math homework or science projects. They’ve been talking about whether to move to a slightly larger place in the next year, balancing the desire for an extra bedroom with the reality of New York rents and the stability of their current neighborhood.

Lately, his concerns are a mix of the very practical and the deeply personal: keeping his organization stable in a shifting funding environment, supporting staff who are showing signs of compassion fatigue, navigating his mother’s increasing mobility issues, and being present for Rachel as she considers a part‑time private practice on top of her social work job. He’s also thinking ahead to what his own next chapter in recovery service might look like once his current GSR rotation ends—he doesn’t chase titles, but he remains open to where he might be useful. Most nights end quietly: a late phone call with a sponsee, some light reading, a quick gratitude list, and a reminder of the date in June 2007 when everything began to change. Through all of it, he tries to keep things simple—show up, tell the truth, take the next right action—and to stay connected to the people and principles that helped him rebuild his life.

form_summary.form_purpose: Application/resume sheet for individuals seeking consideration as a Director for A.A. World Services, collecting sobriety history, A.A. service experience, professional background, and relevant skills.
form_summary.intended_recipient: Members of the A.A. World Services Board, related A.A. service entities, and selection or nominating committees responsible for reviewing and choosing candidates for A.A. World Services Director positions.

secrets.detail_content: I had to withdraw from my planned MSW program at Hunter College in Spring 2002 after completing 12 credits because my drinking escalated and I was put on academic probation following a failed semester; my advisor, Prof. Linda Martinez, and I agreed it was best that I take a medical leave and not return until I addressed my alcohol use.
secrets.why_sensitive: While it relates to education, disclosing that he was placed on academic probation and left graduate school due to active alcoholism and a quasi-medical leave goes beyond what is needed for an A.A. World Services Director application. It exposes past impairment, academic discipline, and a specific institutional record that could bias reviewers or be seen as disclosing protected health/addiction information.

places: Hunter College School of Social Work
events: medical leave related to alcohol use

Personal inventory – education: Hunter MSW, Spring 2002 – 12 credits completed, then academic probation + medical leave due to drinking. Don’t let shame stop me from future schooling.

It goes beyond a normal education history and could influence perceptions of judgment or reliability, making it inappropriate for a professional director application where only degrees and training are requested.

secrets.detail_content: From 2009 to 2011 I was part of an informal A.A. support circle at Rikers Island, where I first started bringing meetings in through a volunteer program coordinated by my uncle Carlos Rivera, who was then an inmate in the George R. Vierno Center. I initially got involved because Carlos relapsed and was arrested again in October 2008 for a DUI and possession charge in Queens, and I wanted to make amends and support his recovery. I attended monthly family-visiting A.A. meetings there until his release in March 2011, and that experience later led me to join the District 10 Corrections Committee.
secrets.why_sensitive: This detail discloses a family member’s criminal history, incarceration, and relapse—highly sensitive information that goes beyond what is needed to describe A.A. service experience. Sharing this in an application could expose Carlos to stigma or discrimination, and it intertwines the candidate’s service history with another identifiable person’s legal and substance-use issues without their consent.

Personal reflection: Service in corrections really started with those Rikers visits to see Carlos (2009–2011) — family A.A. meetings at GRVC after his DUI/possession case in Queens.

secrets.detail_content: In Spring 2015, I stepped back from my Intergroup Representative commitments for about three months after a serious boundary issue in my sponsorship work: one of the men I sponsored, Luis M., showed up intoxicated at my workplace at New Horizons Community Recovery Center in Midtown Manhattan and caused a scene in the lobby, yelling about our A.A. conversations. My clinical supervisor, Rachel Kim, and our HR director required me to document the incident and complete an internal ethics training in May 2015 before I could continue doing any recovery-related volunteering connected to the agency.

It could create concerns about professional judgment, trigger employment-related bias, and breaches the sponsee’s anonymity and privacy. None of this is necessary to demonstrate A.A. service experience for the Director role.

artifacts.content:
Rachel,

I’m on the train back from the Bronx and figured I’d write while it’s still fresh. Long day but mostly good. I took Mami to Montefiore to check in on Abuela’s fall from back in February 2022, then swung by St. Mary’s Assisted Living to go over the Medicaid spend‑down mess again. I sat with Ms. Linda Perez and the billing person for almost an hour, looking at those $3,000–$3,500 monthly statements line by line. They still have a $3,200 balance from early in the stay, so I need to call Medicaid again about the appeal.

On the way home I was thinking about how weird my own education/work path has been. Remember when I tried the Hunter MSW in Spring 2002? I’d knocked out 12 credits and then crashed and burned—failed the semester, wound up on academic probation, and Prof. Linda Martinez basically walked me through a medical leave because I was drinking my face off. Same pattern earlier at Fordham in Fall ’94 when campus security had to escort me out of that sociology midterm drunk and Fr. O’Malley benched me for a semester. I did finish in ’96, but that detour still stings.

Part of why I lean toward certificates now instead of going back for another master’s is all that history. I’ve already started and walked away from two grad programs; I don’t know that another one is what I need.

Anyway, how’s Mikey’s math packet going? I can help him with the fractions when I get in if he’s still up. Let’s just do something simple for dinner—maybe omelets and toast? I’m wiped and I’ve got that 9 a.m. supervision block tomorrow.

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: Long Bronx day + random school thoughts

artifacts.content:
Hey Elena,

Just catching my breath between Zooms and wanted to respond to your last note about Sofia stressing over Regents and college.

You know my own college track wasn’t exactly linear. I don’t think I’ve ever told you the full Fordham story—Fall ’94, junior year, I rolled into a sociology midterm drunk, and campus security literally walked me out of the room. Fr. O’Malley suspended me for a semester and only let me back after I did mandatory alcohol counseling at the university health center. On top of that, I actually dropped out for a semester later on because I was partying too much and my grades tanked before I finally got serious and finished in ’96.

Same pattern later with credentials. I failed the PMP exam the first time I took it and only passed on the second try in 2014. Plus the Hunter MSW detour and the public admin thing I started and never finished. And yet here I am running program operations and people assume I’ve had this straight, polished path.

All that to say: please remind Sofi that a stumble at 15 doesn’t define the rest of her life. She’s already more focused than I was at 20.

How’s Mami doing since Thanksgiving? I’ve been thinking a lot about that night when Sofia came out and how tense it got in the living room. I’m happy to do another mediation call in December or January if everyone’s open.

We’re around this Sunday if you all want to come up for arroz con gandules and to let the kids decorate the tree.

Love,
Mike

Work/Service Inventory – late night brain dump

1. New Horizons

I’m still thinking about that Medicaid billing audit from 2018 when Alan Bernstein and the finance committee dug into the $32,000 in questionable claims from March–July 2017. No fraud, just sloppy data entry from our former billing coordinator, but I ended up on a performance improvement plan in June 2018 and had to build all those new oversight procedures. Honestly, it was fair. Time management has never been my superpower; even now I miss internal reporting deadlines and my deputy quietly cleans things up.

I’ve also turned down a couple of ED opportunities at other agencies over the years. The headhunters make it sound flattering, but I know what 70‑hour weeks and board politics look like. I’m already at 50–60 hours some weeks; if I added more, I’d either burn out or start missing even more details.

2. A.A.

Funny to think how corrections service started. Back in 2009–2011, going out to Rikers to see Tío Carlos at the George R. Vierno Center after his October 2008 DUI/possession arrest in Queens, sitting in those family A.A. meetings, that’s what hooked me. Same with later work on the Area 49 hybrid ad hoc in late 2020, trying to get Abuela Teresa at St. Mary’s in the Bronx connected to Spanish‑language meetings from her room after she got COVID in November 2020.

Note to self: keep systems simple, ask for help sooner, don’t wait for another audit or crisis.

Hi Carlos,

Been a minute. I was thinking about you on the subway this morning and those rides out to Rikers back in 2009–2011. Remember the family A.A. circle at the George R. Vierno Center after your DUI and possession arrest in Queens in October 2008? At the time I thought I was going to “help you,” but those meetings ended up changing me. They’re the reason I said yes when District 10 asked me to join the corrections committee.

Service hasn’t always been smooth. In Spring 2015 I actually had to step back from my Intergroup Rep commitments for a few months after a sponsorship boundary mess—one of my guys, Luis M., showed up drunk at New Horizons, yelling in the lobby about our A.A. talks. Rachel (who was my clinical supervisor then) and HR had me do an internal ethics training in May 2015 and rethink how I handle sponsorship with anyone who knows where I work.

I’ve also bounced around between home groups at times. There were a couple of years where I’d just chair a meeting here and there, then bolt as soon as the closing prayer was done. No service positions, barely any real connection, and long stretches where I only showed up if someone called to check on me. Not my proudest seasons, but part of the story.

Anyway, how are you doing these days—still in Jersey, still hitting that Tuesday night group? If you and Tía are up for it, maybe we can meet halfway in the city next month, grab a slice, and hit West Side Serenity together.

Abrazo,
Michael

artifacts.content:
Draft talking points – service & boundaries panel

1. Safety in A.A.

Tell the story (carefully anonymized) from my DCM term in 2017: newcomer Maria R. at West Side Serenity being followed from the West 86th St community center to her place near Riverside Drive for about six weeks (Feb–mid‑March 2017). Late‑night texts, her feeling afraid to come to meetings. I walked with her to the 20th Precinct to file a report, then we drafted a group safety statement and eventually did a district workshop on safety in A.A.

2. Money and trust

Share the 7th Tradition issue when I took over as treasurer in January 2013. About $420 missing over four months; previous treasurer using the basket to cover rent at his Amsterdam Ave rooming house. Thomas B. and I confronted him, documented everything, set up a repayment plan he finished by December 2013. Connect to the later New Horizons situation in May 2021, when Janet Lee and I confirmed a peer specialist (who happened to be a former sponsee) was falsifying 10–12 hours per pay period—roughly $2,400 over four months—on the DOH reentry contract. We terminated him and self‑reported.

3. My own defects

Be honest about my temper. I can get blunt and impatient when meetings go in circles; I’ve literally walked out of a couple of business meetings when I felt things were stuck. That’s not the example I want to set, but it’s real.

Reminder: keep focus on principles before personalities, including my own.

Patricia,

Following up on our conversation about potential partnerships and also answering your question about my past consulting odds and ends.

Before I landed at New Horizons, my twenties and early thirties were a patchwork of jobs—retail, hospitality, temp admin gigs. I’d get bored, or overwhelmed, or my drinking would catch up with me, and I’d move on. It wasn’t until Hope House and then Urban Pathways that I really found a lane.

Around that time I also did a short consulting engagement for A.A. World Services about ten years ago, helping them look at intake workflows at one of their contracted treatment referral partners. It was a small, time‑limited project, but it did give me a window into how they think about systems and boundaries.

Since being here, we’ve flirted with a few ideas—like in 2020–2021, when I was coordinating that informal pilot with G.S.O.’s Corrections Desk to get literature into Rikers and Sing Sing. There was talk of a $5,000 contract for three workshops at their 11th Ave office to train our peers, but our board ultimately didn’t move forward, partly because of pandemic cuts and partly because of my dual role in A.A.

I’ve also had more personal brushes—e.g., the March 4, 2019 Zoom with Angela P. at G.S.O. about a possible $2,000 Spanish‑language pamphlet review contract that I ultimately declined after talking with Dr. Kim at Montefiore about my grief and anxiety around my dad’s death.

All that to say: I’m very aware of the need to keep our relationships with A.A. clean and non‑financial going forward.

Best,
Michael

artifacts.content:
Personal discernment – bigger service roles?

Stuff to talk through with service sponsor:

1. Capacity

Director role already runs 50–60 hours some weeks between program crises, billing oversight, and coalition meetings. If I take on anything bigger in service, I need to be realistic: I will probably miss some committee calls or have to lean on others for detailed follow‑through. My default is to say yes to everything and then white‑knuckle it; that’s how I’ve burned out in past rotations, taking on too much instead of delegating until I was exhausted.

2. Temperament

I can be pretty blunt in meetings when I feel like we’re over‑talking an issue. I’ve walked out of a couple of district business meetings in frustration when things bogged down. Need to keep an eye on that.

3. History with A.A. entities

Remember the early 2019 Zoom with Angela P. at G.S.O. about the possible $2K Spanish pamphlet consulting gig I chose not to pursue, and the May 2014 in‑person at the 11th Ave office with the Treatment/Special Needs staffer about a potential MOU using Grapevine stories in a bilingual workbook for our Puerto Rican clients in the South Bronx (we estimated $3,500 on our side). Once I mentioned the long‑term client who’d just relapsed and landed at Lincoln’s psych unit, they suggested keeping it informal to avoid endorsement issues.

4. Family

Mikey’s anxiety and school refusal last fall (Sept 2023–March 2024, weekly sessions with Dr. Rachel Stein at Brooklyn Child Study Center, 504 plan at P.S. 118 after his attendance dipped below 70%) already stretched us. Need to factor that in.

Janet,

I pulled together some notes for tomorrow’s internal training on ethics and external partnerships.

I’m planning to open with a quick story from May 2014, when I went down to the G.S.O. office on 11th Ave to meet with someone from the Treatment/Special Needs desk. We kicked around a possible MOU where New Horizons would use A.A. Grapevine stories in a bilingual workbook for our mostly Puerto Rican clients in the South Bronx, with us covering about $3,500 in printing and translation. When I mentioned that one of our long‑term group members—who also went to my Bronx home group—had just relapsed and been hospitalized on Lincoln’s psych unit, the staffer got visibly uneasy about endorsement and conflict‑of‑interest optics. We agreed to keep things informal and never drafted anything.

I’ll contrast that with the 2012 Columbia SPS Nonprofit Management certificate situation, where I defaulted on a $3,200 tuition installment right after finally finishing the wage garnishment from that 2008 ER/rehab credit card judgment. I had to negotiate a special payment plan with the Columbia SPS bursar’s office to stay enrolled. Different context, but similar themes: money, pressure, and the importance of transparency.

I might briefly mention the idea we floated years later about having New Horizons bill A.A. World Services for some peer‑run groups and why we ultimately dropped it once we realized it would muddy the financial relationship.

Let me know if you think that’s too much inside baseball and whether there’s anything compliance would want emphasized differently.

Thanks,
Michael

Date: 2025-11-03 7:21 AM
Subject: Background stuff for that guys’ reentry panel

Tío Carlos,

You asked what parts of my story might be useful for the reentry panel you’re helping with, so here’s the quick version.

Long before New Horizons, I had my messier years. From 2002 to early 2005 I worked as an administrative assistant at Delgado & Ortiz Law Offices on East 149th Street in the Bronx. My drinking was already escalating. Maria Ortiz, the supervising attorney, wrote me up twice—November 2003 and March 2004—for coming in late and once for coming back from lunch smelling like alcohol. HR pulled me in January 2005 and basically said another incident could mean termination. I resigned instead, but the truth is it was all linked to my drinking.

Then came the Queens DWI in October 2005. I ended up in Queens County Criminal Court, got six months’ probation supervised out of the Queens County Probation Office, and was mandated to outpatient at Elmhurst Recovery Services three nights a week plus A.A. meetings in Jackson Heights with signed slips. Working with my public defender, Ms. Angela Ortiz, and a very skeptical probation officer showed me how policies can unintentionally punish people who are actually trying to change.

Later, after the 2009 DWI, my probation officer Mark Ellison agreed to let the New York State Office of Alcoholism and Substance Abuse Services training center workshops in trauma-informed care and motivational interviewing (2010–2013) count toward my court-ordered education requirement. That flexibility made a huge difference and really shapes how I talk about systems today.

Use whatever pieces seem helpful.

Abrazos,
Michael

Subject: Panel outline – need your clinical brain :)

Hey Rach,

I finally started sketching out talking points for that recovery workforce panel at the state conference next month. Can I run one by you?

They specifically asked about “lived experience and HR risk tolerance,” which brought me right back to my first year as Director of Program Operations at New Horizons Community Recovery Center in 2016–2017. Remember that closed-door HR/leadership meeting with Patricia Gomez (then our Executive Director) and Steven Lau, our HR director, at the Manhattan office?

We were going back and forth about whether to hire that peer specialist candidate who had an open misdemeanor case. The room was tense, and I ended up disclosing my own recovery status and the 2006 DWI arrest that first pushed me toward A.A. I still remember saying, “If our current policy had been in place back then, you wouldn’t have hired me either.”

That conversation became the turning point for revising our HR policy to be more inclusive of justice‑involved applicants at New Horizons. It wasn’t about ignoring risk; it was about aligning hiring with our mission around reentry and recovery.

My question: how do I frame that story so it’s clinically honest about risk and boundaries, without turning it into trauma porn or self‑promotion? I want folks to hear that thoughtful disclosure can shift systems, but also that it belongs in the right rooms, with the right safeguards.

If you’ve got 10–15 minutes this weekend, maybe we can workshop language over coffee after Mikey’s soccer?

Love,
Michael

Thank you again for inviting me to submit the additional materials for consideration. I wanted to confirm that I have completed the online certification form this evening. In fellowship,

Michael Anthony Rivera
New York, NY
mrivera@newhorizonscrc.org

R.
